Back Case Study 03
UX Audit Product Design Funnel Redesign Design System

Case Study — Redesign of the Ticket Purchase Flow for the RENFE App

Focus: UX audit, conversion optimization, wireframing and scalable UI architecture across a 7-step purchase funnel.

Challenge
Drop-off in a complex, multi-step purchase funnel
Approach
UX audit, competitive benchmark and funnel-driven redesign
Outcome
Clearer decisions, reduced friction and scalable component system
Role
Product Designer — sole contributor
01 — Context

The RENFE mobile app handles one of Spain's most-used rail ticketing flows — yet its purchase experience presented accumulated friction across search, tariff selection, login, and checkout, directly impacting conversion.

The goal of this conceptual redesign was to optimize the full purchase funnel: simplify the search and selection steps, reduce cognitive load across the checkout, and increase the completion rate while preserving clarity, efficiency, and accessibility.

i

This is a conceptual case study. It reflects an independent design exercise based on publicly available information and does not represent internal RENFE data or an official collaboration.

02 — Funnel & Metrics

The current flow accumulates friction at three critical moments — tariff selection, login, and payment — directly impacting purchase completion.

Critical zones
  • Tariff selection overload — too many simultaneous options
  • Login and extras interruptions breaking the flow mid-funnel
  • Fragmentation between confirmation and payment
Key metrics to track
  • Purchase conversion rate — North Star metric
  • Drop-off rate by funnel phase
  • Average time to complete purchase

The prioritization approach focused on intervening at the highest-impact points for conversion and trust perception, rather than redesigning the full product surface at once.

03 — Research

To ground design decisions in real friction, the approach combined quantitative signals, qualitative feedback, and a competitive benchmark across travel apps.

Quantitative
  • Drop-off rate by phase
  • Time per step
  • Checkout completion rate
  • Filter and change usage
  • Payment error rate
Qualitative
  • App Store reviews and ASO signals
  • Customer support feedback
  • Session recordings (Clarity)
  • Usability tests on tariff selection and payment
Competitive Benchmark
  • Omio, Trainline, 12Go Train
  • Tariff simplification patterns
  • Payment integration approaches
  • Checkout flow management

The combined analysis aimed to detect cognitive barriers, trust issues, and opportunities for simplification — particularly at the moments where users most frequently abandoned the flow.

04 — Synthesis

The analysis revealed accumulated friction at critical funnel moments, particularly around tariff comparison and payment. Two main user profiles emerged with opposing needs.

Frequent traveller
  • Prioritizes speed and efficiency above all
  • Frustrated by repeated mandatory steps
  • Expects remembered preferences and shortcuts
Occasional traveller
  • Needs clarity and confidence at each step
  • Easily overwhelmed by tariff options
  • Sensitive to trust signals and security cues

Opportunities prioritized

  • Simplify tariff selection — fewer options, clearer comparison
  • Maintain context throughout checkout — visible summary always present
  • Reduce key interruptions — move login to a non-blocking step
  • Unify confirmation and payment — collapse two steps into one
05 — Principles

The redesign was grounded in two foundational pillars: accessibility and system consistency. Both were applied across every proposed screen.

Accessibility
  • High contrast and clear visual hierarchy
  • Tactile targets ≥ 44px across all interactive elements
  • Persistent labels and comprehensible microcopy
  • No exclusive reliance on color to communicate states
Consistency & System
  • Reusable patterns for selection and form flows
  • Unified states across all components: error, loading, success
  • Modular components for tariffs and extras
  • Coherent design tokens across spacing, typography and color
i

The objective was to reduce friction through consistent patterns and scalable decisions across the full funnel — not just to fix individual screens.

06 — Proposals

After the research phase, wireframe proposals were developed for seven critical screens of the purchase funnel. Each screen is presented with the issues detected in the current design and the specific improvements introduced in the redesign.

Screen 01
Destination search

Elevated CTA prominence as the clear primary action, icon-based city/station disambiguation, and a guided empty-state for failed searches to prevent abandonment.

Issues detected Current design
High friction Medium friction
  • 01Low-prominence search bar — not perceived as the primary action
  • 02City vs. station ambiguity — causes confusion and input errors
  • 03No feedback on empty search results — leads to abandonment
Proposed wireframe Redesign
  • 01Clear and prioritised CTA — faster funnel entry
  • 02City / station icons — less cognitive load, fewer errors
  • 03Guided "no results" state — clear feedback, less abandonment
Screen 02
Ticket search form

Location-prefilled origin field, a default date set to the following day to eliminate empty states, passenger count with a preset default value, advanced options collapsed under demand, and a unified calendar for outbound/return ranges.

Issues detected Current design
High friction Medium friction
  • 01Too many options at first level — cognitive overload
  • 02Passenger field not actionable — missing default value
  • 03Promo code competes with the main CTA — distraction
  • 04Outbound/return dates unclear — high risk of error
Proposed wireframe Redesign
  • 01Origin prefilled by location — less initial friction
  • 02Default date (next day) — eliminates empty state
  • 03Passengers with default value — better affordance
  • 04Advanced options collapsed — focus on primary action
  • 05Single outbound/return selector — clearer range, fewer errors
Screen 03
Loading screen

Two-tier loading strategy: a skeleton loader for quick searches (1–10 s) and an elevated screen with contextual copy and a cancel option for longer waits (10–30 s). A timeout error state after 30 s prevents complete blocking.

Issues detected Current design
High friction Medium friction
  • 01No clear feedback — high uncertainty for the user
  • 02No user control — feels blocked with no way out
  • 03Animation without progress indication — longer perceived wait
Proposed wireframe Redesign
  • 01Contextual copy for long loads (10–30 s) — reduces anxiety
  • 02Cancel / modify search option — more control, less abandonment
  • 03Skeleton loader for short loads (1–10 s) — reassuring, faster feel
  • 04Error state after 30 s with alternative actions — prevents blocking
Screen 04
Results & tariff selection

Simplified outbound + return flow, filters visible at first level, scarcity indicators, persistent trip summary, simplified tariff cards with optional comparison table, integrated recommendation, and alerts embedded within cards.

Issues detected Current design
High friction Medium friction
  • 01Too many steps in selection — unnecessarily long flow
  • 02Intrusive alerts and upsell — interrupts decision-making
  • 03Complex and repeated tariffs — difficult to compare
  • 04Accidental exit with no warning — progress lost
  • 05Intrusive tariff-upgrade prompts — commercial pressure
  • 06Filters not visible — limited exploration
  • 07Confusing bottom summary — price unclear
Proposed wireframe Redesign
  • 01Simplified outbound + return flow — fewer steps, more efficiency
  • 02Filters visible at first level — faster exploration
  • 03Scarcity indicators — real urgency, faster decision
  • 04Persistent trip summary — continuous context
  • 05Simplified tariff cards + optional comparison table
  • 06Integrated recommendation — guides without friction
  • 07Alerts inside cards — less interruption, continuous flow
Screen 05
Login & passenger data

A three-step checkout with a visible progress bar, non-blocking contextual login, the first passenger section open by default, and clear per-passenger completion states.

Issues detected Current design
High friction Medium friction
  • 01Accidental exit with no security warning — progress lost
  • 02Mandatory login — early barrier in the funnel
  • 03State lost after registration — broken continuity
  • 04Passenger state unclear — leads to more errors
  • 05No sense of progress — higher fatigue and abandonment
Proposed wireframe Redesign
  • 013-step checkout with progress bar — clarity, less uncertainty
  • 02Contextual non-blocking login — continuous flow, less friction
  • 03First passenger open by default — immediate action, clear guidance
  • 04Visible per-passenger states — clear feedback, fewer errors
Screen 06
Extra services

Step integrated into the checkout progress bar, services with visual icons for faster scanning, a non-sticky CTA to ensure full exploration, and services grouped by category.

Issues detected Current design
High friction Medium friction
  • 01Accidental exit with no security warning — progress lost
  • 02Information overload — difficult to read and process
  • 03Extra step in checkout — higher decisional fatigue
Proposed wireframe Redesign
  • 01Step integrated with progress bar — greater clarity
  • 02Visual services with graphic support — faster scanning
  • 03Non-sticky CTA — forced exploration, higher selection rate
  • 04Grouped services — simpler comparison, lower cognitive load
Screen 07
Confirmation & payment

Full consolidation of confirmation and payment into a single scrollable screen — key data always visible, no context switching, and a clear progress indicator at the final step.

Issues detected Current design
High friction Medium friction
  • 01Accidental exit with no security warning — progress lost
  • 02Payment fragmented across multiple steps — longer close
  • 03Context lost at payment — forces user to go back to verify
  • 04Key information not visible enough — higher perceived error
Proposed wireframe Redesign
  • 01Confirmation and payment on a single screen — key data always visible
  • 02Clear progress indicator on final step — less uncertainty
07 — UI Architecture

One screen was developed at high fidelity as a proof of concept for a reusable, scalable component structure — a small design system to ensure visual consistency and support the product's future evolution.

Design tokens (Foundations)

  • Color tokens — primary and neutral scale, consistent across all components
  • Typography tokens — hierarchy, scale, and line-height system

Reusable components

  • Button — Primary, Secondary and Tertiary variants with all interaction states
  • FareCard — modular tariff card for results and comparison screens
  • ActionRow — reusable row pattern for extras and passenger data steps
  • Topbar — persistent header with progress and back navigation
View Design System in Figma
i

All components were built on top of the design tokens, ensuring visual coherence, maintainability, and scalability for future screens and product iterations. The system was designed to be adaptable to new business rules and extendable to other digital channels.

08 — Post-launch

The redesign was scoped with clear hypotheses and measurable metrics to validate each funnel intervention after launch.

Core hypotheses

  • Simplifying tariff selection will reduce decision time and phase-specific drop-off
  • Unifying confirmation and payment will increase checkout completion rate
  • Clarifying tariff copy will reduce plan changes and mid-funnel abandonment
  • Integrating extras without interruption will increase the attach rate
North Star
  • Purchase conversion rate
Secondary metrics
  • Average time to complete purchase
  • Drop-off rate per funnel phase
  • Extras attach rate
  • Payment error rate
  • Search success rate
Learnings

This project reinforced several principles around funnel-focused design and scalable product thinking.

01
Friction is cumulative — fixing one step is not enough.
Each individual pain point may seem manageable, but their combination creates a funnel that consistently breaks at the same moments. Holistic analysis is essential.
02
Default values and progressive disclosure significantly reduce cognitive load.
Prefilled fields, sane defaults, and collapsing advanced options let users focus on what matters — and move faster when they already know what they want.
03
A design system isn't overhead — it's how quality scales.
Building reusable tokens and components from the start ensures that new screens inherit the same visual logic without requiring rework from scratch each time.
04
Designing for two user profiles simultaneously forces better decisions.
Balancing the frequent traveller's need for speed against the occasional traveller's need for clarity produces interfaces that serve both — and neither feels neglected.
